assertiveness_why: Harry asks prepared, structured multi-part questions and follows up for clarification, but never challenges or contests any of Michael's answers.
question_style: Mostly open, well-prepared questions that build logically through a fixed list of topics (background, market sizing, founder traits, quickfire); Harry occasionally asks a clarifying follow-up ('do you really deep dive on future market size?') but never pushes back or interrupts.
tone: Formal and polite, deferential to the guest as an authority, enthusiastic reactions ('Absolutely,' 'Amazing, amazing') but no self-deprecation or matey banter.
depth: Mostly surface-level and definitional (market sizing philosophy, stage-agnostic investing, founder tells) with light follow-up on future vs. present market sizing; no technical/product depth.
harry_quotes: How big does the market have to be to make it an attractive investment for you?
Do you really deep dive on the future market size, or do you just look at present?
Do VCs like this simplification of business models?
learnings: Accel evaluates opportunity size rather than raw market size, since the addressable slice is always much smaller than the total market.
Founder domain fluency matters more in complex/regulated verticals like insurance than in simpler consumer categories.
A VC's value-add compounds over time through pattern recognition, network introductions, and hiring support rather than one-off advice.
Enterprise software is converging with consumer UX because employees are the same people who are consumers outside work.
notable: Includes a fixed 'quickfire round' segment (productivity tools, tech hub predictions, favorite book) that later becomes a recurring 20VC format staple.
